narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and undergraduate GPA. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 69% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants most likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling a notable dip in performance that outweighed other positives. Your LSAT score, slightly below the typical strong performer, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing but not enough to offset the concerns. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, while respectable, was lower than average and provided only moderate support for success. Your Year 1 decile in the lower-middle range added a small nudge toward the failure prediction, while your middle family income bracket and full-time student status offered minor positive influences. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later academic deciles stand out as weaker, even as a full-time student like most (over 90%) in the dataset. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law, but rather the model's focus on these patterns from past bar-takers. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year grades, aiming for top deciles, and perhaps supplementing with LSAT prep or GPA-improving post-bac work. Reapply after strengthening these areas, and seek mentorship from successful Black lawyers who can guide your path. You've got this—many have overcome similar hurdles to thrive.
